Use this for pure computation with no I/O. Under the hood it maps across objects.: runs once and returns the same value for all objects. Useful for computed aggregates or context-dependent fields.: shorthands for common ActiveRecord patterns, including association loading and record fetching by ID.The gains for list-heavy queries mirror what they reported. 15x faster execution and 75% less memory are not unusual at high repetition. Flat queries with no lists will see little difference. The benefit scales with how much your responses repeat field patterns across objects.Shopify closes their post with a direct quote from the official GraphQL specification: conformance requirements can be fulfilled “in any way as long as the perceived result is equivalent.”Breadth-first execution is spec-compliant. The spec never mandated depth-first traversal. The community converged on it. Depth-first is the practical choice for most GraphQL APIs today. The migration cost to breadth-first isn’t justified when list sizes are small and the gains are negligible. But the assumption that it’s the only execution model worth considering is harder to defend now.The challenge for the broader community is practical. Most GraphQL tooling, documentation, and resolver patterns are built around the depth-first interface. A migration requires a resolver interface change that touches every field in a codebase. Their tooling (interpreter bridges, shadow verifiers, AI-assisted migration) represents significant investment in making that transition safe at scale.Whether teams outside Shopify can make a similar transition without similar resources is an open question. What’s no longer open is whether the execution model is worth questioning.

item.title: The CBDC Promise Isn’t Wrong. The Execution Usually Is
item.author: Vitalii Yatskiv
item.content:encoded: On financial inclusion, government mandates, and why 1.3 billion people are still outside the formal economy despite owning smartphones.There’s a number that keeps surfacing in conversations about financial infrastructure, and it deserves more scrutiny than it typically gets.\ 1.3 billion adults worldwide have no financial account. According to the 2025 World Bank Global Findex Database, 86% of those same adults own a mobile phone. They have a device, a SIM card, and in most cases a government-issued ID. What they’re missing is not hardware. It’s a product simple and cheap enough to be worth using, or an institution they have reason to trust.\ That gap is what Central Bank Digital Currencies are theoretically designed to close. Whether they actually will depends almost entirely on choices that have nothing to do with the technology.Why cash is harder to replace than anyone expectedThe unbanked don’t use cash out of ignorance. They use it because it works reliably where other products have failed them.Think about what cash actually offers: no transaction fees, no minimum balance requirement, no documentation to use, and it functions without internet. It cannot be frozen by a third party. No digital financial product in mass deployment today matches all five of those properties at once, and that is not a coincidence.Every fintech product that has targeted the unbanked over the last decade has eventually hit the same wall. The overhead of building and maintaining infrastructure for low-value transactions in low-connectivity regions is not commercially attractive. Private operators enter, discover the unit economics don’t work, and either exit or migrate upmarket. The people they were supposed to serve remain where they were.This is a structural problem, not a product problem. And it’s why a public-sector instrument with a different incentive structure, one that doesn’t need to generate a return on equity, is genuinely worth examining.What CBDCs actually offerA CBDC issued by a central bank carries one structural advantage that no private product can replicate: it is a direct liability of the sovereign. For populations that have watched private financial institutions fail, impose predatory fees, or simply refuse to serve them, that credibility matters more than any feature comparison.Three specific design properties make CBDCs relevant to inclusion.Offline transaction capability. A significant portion of the unbanked population lives in areas with unreliable or absent internet connectivity. CBDCs designed with short-range communication protocols can settle transactions without network access, functionally replicating what cash does today. This is not a theoretical capability; it is being actively developed in several central bank pilots. Whether it survives the procurement and deployment process is another matter.Near-zero transaction costs. The overhead of legacy banking, branch networks, correspondent relationships, compliance layers, is the primary reason small-value transactions in underserved markets have never been commercially viable. A CBDC that strips those layers out can make a fifty-cent transaction economically rational in a way that traditional banking never could.A path to credit identity. This is the most underappreciated capability. The reason financial institutions classify the entire unbanked population as high-risk is not prejudice; it is the absence of data. No transaction history means no risk model, which means no credit, no insurance, no formal financial participation. CBDC transaction data, shared voluntarily with consent, creates a financial identity from nothing. One year of consistent payment behavior becomes the foundation for a loan evaluation. That is not incremental. That is a structural change in who can access capital.IMF and BIS research shows that approximately 60% of emerging and low-income countries list financial inclusion as one of their top three motivations for issuing a CBDC. That is a strong stated priority.It is also where the optimism should pause.Most CBDCs currently in development or deployment are not designed for the unbanked. They are designed for the already-banked, as more efficient alternatives to existing payment infrastructure. Offline functionality gets deprioritized. Distribution gets routed through commercial banks, the same institutions that already determined this demographic was not worth serving. Privacy-preserving data sharing mechanisms, which are the actual engine of credit inclusion, are rarely the priority they should be.The gap between stated intent and design reality is not accidental. It reflects whose interests are most represented in the development process. Central banks sit at the intersection of government mandate and institutional finance. The unbanked do not have a lobby.What this looks like in emerging marketsIn markets across Central Asia and Eastern Europe, the financial inclusion gap looks different on the ground than it does in policy papers. Mobile penetration is high, national digital identity infrastructure is developing rapidly, and the formal banking system has historically been built around urban populations and state-connected enterprises.The conditions for CBDC-driven inclusion are arguably better in these regions than in the countries that dominate the policy conversation. The populations are digitally ready. The existing system has left obvious gaps. Political will to modernize financial infrastructure exists, partly because regulatory clarity around digital assets is advancing faster in some of these jurisdictions than in Western Europe.What is missing, in most cases, is the willingness to make design choices that actually serve inclusion rather than institutional efficiency. That is a governance problem more than a technology problem.The 1.3 billion number will not move because CBDCs exist. It will move when specific design decisions are made: offline-first architecture, non-bank distribution partners embedded in underserved communities, consent-based transaction data sharing with formal credit markets.Those decisions require governments to prioritize the financially excluded over institutional stakeholders during procurement and design. That has not been the default outcome in most deployments so far.The technology is ready. The mandate exists in most cases. The gap between the two is political, not technical, and that gap is precisely where the next decade of financial infrastructure will be won or lost.

item.title: Medical AI Сan Diagnose. But Сan It Explain?
item.author: Marat Davudov
item.content:encoded:
Imagine this: an AI system suggests a diagnosis — but cannot explain why. In many areas of technology, that might be acceptable. In healthcare, it isn’t.Over the past decade, machine learning systems have achieved impressive results in clinical tasks. have shown that their diagnostic accuracy is comparable to that of physicians, particularly in tasks involving well-structured data such as medical imaging.But this progress comes with a less obvious trade-off. As models become more accurate, they also become more complex — and, as a result, harder to understand. The same properties that make them powerful often make them difficult to interpret.And that’s not just a technical inconvenience.Medical decisions need to be justified. They are discussed with colleagues, explained to patients, and sometimes challenged or revised. A system that cannot explain its outputs doesn’t just feel uncomfortable — it simply doesn’t fit into how medicine actually works.This is exactly the problem I encountered while working on automatic medical coding. Modern models can read long discharge summaries and assign diagnosis codes in seconds. At first glance, the task appears solved. But as soon as you ask how those predictions can be verified, it becomes clear that accuracy alone is not enough.This led me to a broader conclusion. Interpretability in medical AI is not a feature or a nice-to-have. It is a fundamental requirement that determines whether a system can be understood, validated, and ultimately used in real clinical practice.To understand what this means in reality, we first need to clarify what interpretability actually means in a clinical setting.1. What Interpretability Actually Means in Medical AIA common misconception is that interpretability is simply about making models more transparent.That framing sounds reasonable — but it’s wrong.In a clinical setting, the question is not how a model works internally, but whether its output can be justified. A prediction is only useful if it can be connected to concrete evidence and evaluated within the framework of clinical decision-making logic.This shifts the focus from transparency to justification. Interpretability is not about exposing the inner workings of a model, but about making its outputs understandable in terms that can be verified against the patient’s data.This becomes especially clear in real workflows.Consider the task of documenting a patient’s diagnoses over the course of a hospital stay. This typically requires reading thousands of words of clinical text and extracting all relevant conditions. Modern machine learning models can generate that list in seconds.But this raises a critical question: how can a clinician verify that predictions such as “Type II Diabetes” or “Atrial Fibrillation” are correct without re-reading the entire discharge note?If verification requires reading the full document again, automation quickly becomes useless.This is why an interpretable system must do more than output predictions — it must show the evidence behind them. If a model predicts “Type II Diabetes,” it should point to specific phrases such as “poorly controlled diabetes” or “on metformin” that justify the decision.This is where an important distinction emerges. It is not enough to highlight statistically important parts of the input. The explanation must align with clinical reasoning and serve as actual evidence, not just signal importance.Otherwise, interpretability becomes superficial — the system appears explainable, but does not help the clinician verify or challenge its output.In this sense, interpretability is not a property of the model alone. It is a property of the system as a whole — how its outputs are presented, whether they can be evaluated, and whether they support real decision-making in practice.2. Why is interpretability crucial in medical diagnosis?Why does it matter that the outputs of an AI system can be inspected and challenged?Because in medicine, decisions do not end with a prediction. They are communicated, questioned, and often revised — and even when a model is involved, responsibility remains with the clinician. This creates a simple but strict constraint: you cannot act on a decision you cannot explain.Clinical practice is not a series of isolated decisions, but an ongoing process. Diagnoses are discussed with colleagues, handed over between teams, reviewed through second opinions, and revisited over time. They are also explained to patients, and those explanations directly affect consent and trust. If a system cannot fit into this process, it remains an isolated tool — even if it is highly accurate.Interpretability makes this integration possible. It allows clinicians to understand what a model’s decision is based on, communicate it to others, and question it when something seems wrong. This is not just about trust — it is about having a mechanism to detect and correct errors. When a prediction looks suspicious, there must be a way to trace what led to it.There is also a practical dimension. High accuracy is not enough. In real-world settings, models will inevitably produce outputs that conflict with clinical reasoning or established guidelines. At that point, clinicians need to decide whether the model has identified a meaningful exception or whether it is simply wrong. Without interpretability, that decision becomes guesswork.Another layer is ethics and fairness. Medical data reflects real-world biases: missing information, uneven representation across patient groups, and historical inequalities. Models can reproduce — or even amplify — these patterns. Interpretability makes this integration possible. It allows clinicians to understand what drives a model’s decision, communicate it to others, and challenge it when something looks wrong. This isn’t just about trust. It’s about having a mechanism to detect and correct errors. When a prediction doesn’t make sense, there must be a way to trace the evidence behind it.All of this leads to two practical consequences. If AI systems influence decisions about individuals, there is an increasing expectation that those decisions can be explained. This is already being formalised in legal frameworks. Systems that cannot be understood are difficult to trust and impossible to defend. In practice, such tools are often not used, especially when they conflict with guidelines or cannot be audited for bias.Ultimately, interpretability is not just a model property. It is what determines whether a system can actually be used in real-world healthcare.To explore this in practice, I focused on a concrete task: automatic assignment of ICD-9 diagnosis codes to patient discharge summaries. This is a challenging setting, involving long clinical texts, domain-specific language, and thousands of possible labels. Even models that perform well on this task are typically complex and difficult to interpret.For this analysis, I used the MSMN (Multi-Synonym Matching Network) model trained on the MIMIC-III dataset, which is a widely used benchmark for medical coding tasks. The dataset contains de-identified records from more than 40,000 ICU patients, including discharge summaries paired with ground-truth ICD codes.Each note is on average 1500–1700 words long, and the full label space includes more than 8,000 possible codes. To make the task more manageable, a common approach researchers take is to restrict the problem to the top 50 most frequent codes, and this is what I do as well.\ As can be seen from the figure above, MSMN doesn’t show the best possible performance on the ICD coding task. However, I chose it because it still shows strong performance and is representative of a broader class of attention-based models used in medical NLP, while still being lightweight enough to analyse in detail.3. What Methods Were ExploredOnce we define interpretability as the ability to connect predictions to evidence and reasoning used to generate them, the next question becomes practical: how do we actually extract that evidence from a model?In this work, I focused on two approaches: attention-based explanations and Integrated Gradients. They answer similar questions, but from very different angles.The first approach comes directly from the model itself — attention scores.Although attention is often associated with transformer models like GPT (the model behind chatbot LLMs like ChatGPT), the underlying idea is more general. Put simply, attention helps the model focus on different parts of the input depending on the task.In the context of clinical text, this is particularly useful. Discharge summaries are long and information-dense, and contain both critical details and irrelevant noise. A model needs to identify which parts of the text actually support a given diagnosis.MSMN approaches this in a specific way. Instead of using transformer-style self-attention, it first encodes the text using a bi-directional LSTM, and then applies a label-specific attention mechanism. The key difference is that the model is not asking “which words relate to each other,” but rather “which words are relevant to this particular ICD code.”To make this work, each ICD code is extended with a set of synonyms derived from medical ontologies. Each synonym effectively acts as a query over the encoded text. This produces attention scores for each word, indicating how relevant that word is to the code or one of its clinical descriptions.These scores can be extracted after prediction and used as a form of explanation. If the model predicts a diabetes-related code, attention allows us to inspect whether it focused on phrases like “diabetes,” “metformin,” or “poor glycaemic control,” rather than unrelated parts of the note.However, there is an important limitation. While MSMN internally computes these relevance scores, it does not expose them in a directly usable form. Extracting meaningful explanations requires additional processing, and even then, attention only tells us where the model focused — not necessarily what actually drove the prediction.This leads to the second approach: Integrated Gradients.Unlike attention, this method does not depend on the model’s architecture. It provides a way to estimate how much each input feature contributes to the final prediction.The idea is to compare the model’s output on the real input to its output on a baseline — in this case, an empty sequence of padding tokens. By gradually moving from the baseline to the real input and tracking how the prediction changes, we can attribute importance scores to individual words.In the context of clinical text, this allows us to estimate how strongly each word influenced the assignment of a particular ICD code. Instead of asking where the model is focusing, Integrated Gradients asks a different question: how much does each word actually contribute to the decision? 
 
 This distinction is important.Attention provides a view into the model’s internal weighting of the input. Integrated Gradients, on the other hand, attempts to measure how each token contributes to the prediction — what would change if a particular word were not present.Both approaches, therefore, offer useful but incomplete perspectives.Attention is naturally available in models like MSMN and is relatively easy to extract, but it may highlight signals that are not truly decisive. Integrated Gradients is more general and theoretically grounded, but requires additional computation and depends on the choice of baseline.Together, they provide two complementary ways of looking at the same problem: one focused on relevance, the other on contribution.Now that we have two different ways of extracting explanations, the next question is straightforward: how do we compare them?Ideally, we would evaluate them qualitatively. That would mean taking explanations produced by both attention scores and Integrated Gradients for the same clinical notes, and asking trained professionals to assess which ones are more useful or more aligned with clinical reasoning.In practice, however, this kind of evaluation is expensive and difficult to scale.As a result, we need alternative ways to compare the methods — specifically, quantitative metrics that can serve as a proxy for explanation quality when direct human evaluation is not available.\ A simple way to approach this is to look at the most important words identified by each method and compare them to the actual description of the predicted ICD code.For each prediction, we can take the top-k words ranked by importance — whether from attention scores or Integrated Gradients — and check how well they align with the clinical meaning of the code.Based on this idea, I defined two quantitative metrics.Percentage of direct word matches between important words and code descriptions.This metric measures how often the most important words identified by the model explicitly appear in the ICD code description.To make this more robust, I removed common stop words such as “a”, “the”, and “but”, which would otherwise inflate the match rate without adding meaningful signal.Correlation between importance scores and presence in the code description.This metric looks at whether higher importance scores actually correspond to clinically relevant words.To do this, I treat the importance score of each word — whether from attention or Integrated Gradients — as a predictor for a binary outcome: whether that word appears in the ICD code description or not.Based on this setup, we can compute standard classification metrics such as AUROC and Average Precision (AP). These metrics help quantify how well each method ranks clinically meaningful words above irrelevant ones.I computed these two metrics across a large set of discharge notes and corresponding MSMN predictions under three different configurations.Top-5 words (with repetition).In this setup, I selected the top-k words with the highest importance scores, allowing repetitions if the same word appeared multiple times in the note and was consistently assigned high importance.To control for potential bias towards frequently occurring terms, I repeated the experiment using only unique words. Each word could appear at most once in the list of top terms, even if it was present multiple times in the text.As a reference point, I also included all words from the clinical note, regardless of their importance scores. This provides a baseline for how much overlap we would expect without any ranking signal.5. What Did the Results Show?Here are the results across both evaluation metrics.Direct matching (top-k important words vs ICD descriptions):How well do importance scores predict whether a word belongs to the code description:\ Across all configurations, Integrated Gradients consistently produces explanations that are better aligned with the meaning of the predicted ICD codes than attention scores.The difference is not dramatic — but it is consistent. And that matters.It suggests that explanation methods are not interchangeable, even when applied to the same model and the same predictions.At a high level, the results support a simple intuition:Attention shows where the model is lookingIntegrated Gradients captures what actually influences the prediction 
 In practice, this difference shows up in the extracted evidence. Integrated Gradients tends to surface tokens that are more directly tied to the clinical meaning of the code, while attention is more likely to highlight contextually relevant but less informative parts of the note.At the same time, these results should not be overinterpreted.This evaluation is still a proxy. It measures alignment with code descriptions, not true clinical usefulness. Ultimately, the quality of an explanation depends on whether it helps a human understand and validate the model’s decision in context.So the conclusion is not that Integrated Gradients “solves” interpretability — but that it provides a more reliable signal than attention in this setting.6. What This Means in PracticeThe main takeaway is not about choosing between Attention and Integrated Gradients.It is about how interpretability should be treated when building real systems.A model can achieve strong benchmark performance and still be unusable in practice if its predictions cannot be inspected and verified. In this project, that meant adding explanations on top of a strong model, and then critically evaluating whether those explanations actually made sense.This leads to a broader point:Interpretability is not a property of the model — it is a property of the system.You are not just building a predictor. You are building something that a human needs to interact with, question, and ultimately rely on.And this is where implementation matters, as this requires an extra layer (or multiple) on top of the model building, requiring extra effort and time to configure, but it’s arguably the most important part of the pipeline.To explore this in practice, I built a simple interactive demo available on . It allows you to inspect model predictions alongside highlighted evidence generated by different explanation methods and see how they behave in real scenarios.The goal is not to prove that one method is definitively better, but to make the behaviour of these explanations visible and testable.Because in the end, that is the real test.Not whether an explanation scores well on a metric — but whether a human can look at it and say:“I understand why this prediction was made.”If your system cannot support that, then no amount of benchmark performance will make it trustworthy.

item.title: How I Cut Our Cloud Bill by 40% Without Touching a Single Data Model
item.author: JEEVAN KRISHNA PARUCHURI
item.content:encoded: \ The Slack message from my manager was three words long. 'We need to talk.' That's how I found out our Azure spend had crossed a number that was making people uncomfortable in the finance review.I'd been expecting it, honestly. We'd been scaling fast (more pipelines, more datasets, more teams querying the lake) and I hadn't sat down to seriously look at where the money was going. That was a mistake. When I finally pulled the cost breakdown, the culprit wasn't what I expected.My first assumption was that we were over-provisioning Spark clusters. That's usually the go-to answer when cloud bills spike. Someone left a fat cluster running, or the auto-scaling config is too aggressive. I spent half a day pulling cluster utilisation metrics and honestly, they weren't bad. Average CPU was around 60%, memory was fine. That wasn't it.The real problem was storage. Specifically: 18 months of accumulated small files, expired snapshots we'd never cleaned up, and a Spark streaming job that had been writing 200KB Parquet files into Delta Lake at roughly 4-minute intervals since the day it went live. We had tables with half a million files in them. Running even a basic aggregation on those tables was slow not because of compute but because of the metadata overhead. The driver was spending more time building the file list than running the query.The storage bill looked fine on its own. But the compute bill for querying badly maintained storage was enormous. That's the part that doesn't show up clearly in cost dashboards until you're looking for it.The three things we actually fixedI want to be specific here because vague advice about 'optimising your storage layer' is everywhere and mostly useless. Here's what we actually did.First: compaction. We set up a weekly Spark job that runs OPTIMIZE on every Delta table in our processed zone, targeting a target file size of 256MB. For tables that receive continuous streaming writes, we run it nightly. This dropped the file count on our worst tables from around 480,000 files to under 2,000. Query performance on those tables improved by roughly 6x. The compute cost for the compaction job itself is trivial compared to what we were spending querying fragmented storage. (A note on versions: we were running Delta Lake 2.1 on Spark 3.3 at the time. OPTIMIZE behaviour, particularly around Z-ordering and bin-packing thresholds, has changed across versions, so check the documentation for your specific version.)Second: snapshot expiry. Delta Lake keeps a history of all table versions by default, and we had never configured retention. Some tables had 14 months of snapshots sitting in storage doing nothing. We set a 30-day retention policy across the board and ran VACUUM. I won't tell you the exact GB we freed up because honestly the number is embarrassing given how long we let it accumulate. It was a lot.Third: right-sizing the streaming write interval. The job that was writing tiny files every 4 minutes was doing it because that's what the original author set and no one had questioned it. The downstream consumers didn't need data more frequently than every 30 minutes. We changed the trigger interval. File sizes went from 200KB average to 180MB average. Done.Before and after: our worst table went from ~480,000 files averaging 200KB to under 2,000 files averaging 180MB. Snapshot history dropped from 14 months to 30 days. Streaming write interval moved from 4 minutes to 30 minutes. Total query time on affected tables improved by roughly 6x.Those three changes accounted for the bulk of the savings. But I also made a mistake in what I tried next, and it’s worth mentioning because the instinct behind it was reasonable.What I got wrong in the first passMy first instinct after the compaction job ran was to immediately move to tiered storage: archive cold partitions to the cool or archive tier, keep only the last 90 days in hot. That logic made sense on paper but I underestimated how often 'cold' data was still being queried. We have a compliance requirement that means audit teams pull 2-3 year old transaction data fairly regularly. Archiving those partitions would have hurt query latency on regulatory lookups, which is exactly the wrong thing to optimise away in a banking environment.I put tiered storage on hold and instead focused on partitioning strategy. Several of our large tables were partitioned by ingestion date rather than business date. That meant queries filtering by transaction date, which is most queries, were doing full scans across partition boundaries. Re-partitioning those tables was a bigger project, but the long-term compute savings justified it.The 40% reduction took about three months from the first compaction run to showing up meaningfully in the monthly bill. The breakdown was roughly: 15% from compaction and query efficiency gains, 12% from snapshot cleanup reducing storage costs, 8% from the streaming file size fix, and the remaining 5% from a few other smaller adjustments we made along the way.We didn't touch the data models. We didn't change schemas. We didn't ask downstream analysts to rewrite queries. The business didn't notice anything except that some reports started running faster.One thing worth saying plainlyNone of this required exotic tooling or architectural heroics. Everything I've described is available in Delta Lake's standard API and is documented. The reason we hadn't done it was not ignorance. Nobody owned it. Compaction, snapshot management, file size hygiene: these are operational disciplines, not one-time tasks. We now have a dedicated maintenance pipeline that runs on a schedule, with alerting if table health metrics fall outside acceptable ranges.If your cloud bill is growing faster than your data volume, the answer is usually sitting in your storage layer. Pull the file count histograms first. The rest tends to follow.

item.title: Why Cloud Monitoring Has Become K–12’s Most Critical Cyber Defense Tool
item.author: Charlie Sander
item.content:encoded: It starts with a simple student login: one account gets phished, a file is dropped into a shared drive, and within minutes, malware has synced and spread across the entire network. By the time IT teams notice, the damage is already systemic – not because their defenses failed, but because the very tools meant for optimization that they trust are accelerating the attack.This is the new reality of K-12 cybersecurity in 2026: cloud sync has become ransomware’s most efficient delivery mechanism. Popular platforms like Google Workspace and Microsoft 365 were built for speed and access, but their cloud sync technologies can expose school districts to attacks that move faster than any human response team can match.According to the 2025 COS MS-ISAC Report, of schools faced cyber incidents last year, most of which spread through under-supervised background activity and automated cloud-sync technologies. Meanwhile, the 2024 Educational Cybersecurity Report that K-12 schools experience three times as many security incidents per student as any other sector, stemming from activity within trusted environments.The financial impact is equally devastating, with the average cost of a data breach in education reaching $4.88 million in 2024 – a 15% increase from the previous year.Effective cybersecurity is not necessarily about overhauling entire networks, but about improving security controls and expanding visibility reach. Without real-time monitoring of cloud apps like Gmail, Outlook, shared drives, and collaborative documents, schools are flying blind while the average recovery cost reaches .Security strategies are still being built for traditional networks, but the risk now lives in SaaS ecosystems.The new K-12 data ecosystemNetworks rely heavily on cloud sync technologies to facilitate group flow and data use. Yet, features such as live synchronization and network-wide configurations can unintentionally accelerate the spread of malware. These technologies automatically replicate data across databases and spread compromised files throughout the network ecosystem.Attackers, in fact, increasingly exploit legitimate access to platforms, gaining easy access via cookies and permissions. Compromised credentials and service accounts allow them to move freely across systems, accessing sensitive data without user intervention, and potentially critical verification standpoints.APIs compound the problem further, as they enable automated data exchange between apps without any user intervention, removing critical verification checkpoints that might otherwise catch anomalous behavior.Because modern malicious activity is designed to blend in, file changes don’t always trigger endpoint alerts, OAuth connections bypass traditional controls, and third-party apps operate inside trusted environments. The threat is genuinely difficult to distinguish from normal user activity.One of the emerging threats for 2026 is the tools that operate silently inside school networks, nearly invisible to IT teams. In K-12 environments, teachers and students are using AI tools that process sensitive academic, health, and financial data outside IT oversight, where some of these platforms often work through hidden activity that spreads quickly.The core problem with Shadow AI is limited visibility. IT teams lose the ability to track where data flows, how long it sits in third-party systems, and who it’s actively shared with. Such a blind spot heightens risks of phishing, ransomware, and FERPA noncompliance.Due to their integration through OAuth permissions, which allow them to handle and keep sensitive data, the decentralized and unregulated use of AI tools in classrooms has recently increased exposure to the problem. Without IT workers' awareness or control, these tools may unintentionally spread throughout vast amounts of data.K-12 districts are particularly attractive targets because the data they hold—student records, health information, financial data, academic IDs—command high prices in underground markets. The value, combined with reduced budgets and limited IT resources, makes schools an efficient target. An effective solution, however, is not entirely cutting the use of AI, but rather setting up guardrails and human oversight systems. An IT team can easily identify AI inside an environment through pattern recognition and tracking down file sharing across the network.Continuous cloud monitoringShifting to continuous cloud monitoring gives district IT teams more meaningful control over their Google and Microsoft environments by monitoring cloud-sync systems and detecting and responding to threats before they escalate.Cloud monitoring connects directly to cloud platforms via APIs to analyze activity and identify patterns triggered by malware and Shadow AI. Rather than relying solely on endpoint protections or sudden alerts, they can detect malware through pattern recognition and provide timely prevention to stop its spread.When this activity is then evaluated against behavioral baselines and predefined risk indicators, it becomes easy to identify, flag, and respond to unusual activity. And, in practice, this approach shifts security from reactive firefighting to proactive control against threats, thereby protecting entire networks from threats before their impact hits.The missing operational layer in modern K–12 cybersecurityAs pandemic-era , districts have to act efficiently with their available resources. IT teams can remain lean, even as the complexity and volume of cloud-based threats continue to grow. It's no longer about damage control, but about damage .Cloud monitoring becomes an efficiency tool rather than just a security investment by reducing response times, limiting operational disruption, and helping teams stay ahead of issues before they escalate. And, in cloud-first environments, an additional question is no longer about how quickly they recover following a cyber attack, but whether they can detect and stop it before it rampages through trusted systems.Visibility is thus no longer optional; it’s foundational.

item.title: New Gas-Powered Data Centers Could Emit More Greenhouse Gases Than Entire Nations
item.author: BeauHD
item.content:encoded:
An anonymous reader quotes a report from Wired: New gas projects linked to just 11 data center campuses around the US have the potential to create more greenhouse gases than the country of Morocco emitted in 2024. Emissions estimates from air permit documents examined by WIRED show that these natural gas projects -- which are being built to power data centers to serve some of the US's most powerful AI companies, including OpenAI, Meta, Microsoft, and xAI -- have the potential to emit more than 129 million tons of greenhouse gases per year. As tech companies race to secure massive power deals to build out hundreds of data centers across the country, these projects represent just the tip of the iceberg when it comes to the potential climate cost of the AI boom.
The infrastructure on this list of large natural gas projects reviewed by WIRED is being developed to largely bypass the grid and provide power solely for data centers, a trend known as behind-the-meter power. As data center developers face long waits for connections to traditional utilities, and amid mounting public resistance to the possibility of higher energy bills, making their own power is becoming an increasingly popular option. These projects have either been announced or are under construction, with companies already submitting air permit application materials with state agencies. [...] The emissions projections for the xAI and Microsoft projects, and all the others on WIRED's list, were pulled directly from publicly-available air permit documents in state databases as well as public air permit materials collected by both Cleanview and Oil and Gas Watch, a database maintained by the Environmental Integrity Project, an environmental enforcement nonprofit. Actual greenhouse gas emissions from power plants are usually lower than what's on their air permits. Air permit modeling is based on the scenario of a power plant constantly running at full capacity. That's rarely the reality for grid-connected power plants, as turbines go offline for maintenance or adjust to the ebbs and flows of customer demand.
"Permitted emission numbers represent a theoretical, conservative scenario, not the actual projected emissions," Alex Schott, the director of communications at Williams Companies, an oil and gas company that is building out three behind-the-meter power plants in Ohio for Meta, told WIRED in an email. Internal modeling done by the company, Schott added, shows that actual emissions could be "potentially two-thirds less than what's on paper." The projections involved, however, are still substantial. Even if the actual emissions from these power plants end up being half of the emissions numbers on the permits, they still could create more greenhouse gas emissions than the country of Norway emitted in 2024. This number is, according to the EPA, equivalent to the emissions from more than 153 average-sized natural gas plants. (WIRED's analysis does not include emissions from backup generators and turbines on the data center campuses themselves, which create smaller amounts of emissions.) Energy researcher Jon Koomey says the data center boom has created a shortage of the most efficient gas turbines, pushing some developers toward less efficient models that would need to run longer and produce more emissions. "[Data center operators'] belief is that the value being delivered by the servers is much, much more than the cost of running these inefficient power plants all the time," he said.
Michael Thomas, the founder of clean energy research firm Cleanview, has been tracking gas permits for data centers across the country. He calls behind-the-meter power "a crazy acceleration of emissions." He added: "It's almost like we thought we were on the downside of the Industrial Revolution, retiring coal and gas, and now we have a new hump where we're going to rise. That terrifies me in a lot of ways."

item.title: Apple Stops Weirdly Storing Data That Let Cops Spy On Signal Chats
item.author: BeauHD
item.content:encoded:
Apple has fixed a bug that could cause parts of Signal notifications to remain stored on iPhones even after messages disappeared and the app was deleted. "Affected users concerned about push notifications can update their devices to stop what Apple characterized as 'notifications marked for deletion' that 'could be unexpectedly retained on the device,'" reports Ars Technica. "According to Apple, the push notifications should never have been stored, but a 'logging issue' failed to redact data." From the report: Vulnerable users hoping to evade law enforcement surveillance often use encrypted apps like Signal to communicate sensitive information. That's why users felt blindsided when 404 Media reported that Apple was unexpectedly storing push notifications displaying parts of encrypted messages for up to a month. This occurred even after the message was set to disappear and the app itself was deleted from the device.
404 Media flagged the issue after speaking to multiple people who attended a hearing where the FBI testified that it "was able to forensically extract copies of incoming Signal messages from a defendant's iPhone, even after the app was deleted, because copies of the content were saved in the device's push notification database." The shocking revelation came in a case that 404 Media noted was "the first time authorities charged people for alleged 'Antifa' activities after President Trump designated the umbrella term a terrorist organization." "We're grateful to Apple for the quick action here, and for understanding and acting on the stakes of this kind of issue," Signal's post said. "It takes an ecosystem to preserve the fundamental human right to private communication."
In their post, Signal confirmed that after users update their devices, "no action is needed for this fix to protect Signal users on iOS. Once you install the patch, all inadvertently-preserved notifications will be deleted and no forthcoming notifications will be preserved for deleted applications."

item.title: Redwood Materials loses COO amid layoffs, restructuring

item.content:encoded: Former Tesla executive Chris Lister is retiring, and at least three other VPs have left Redwood around the recent restructuring, TechCrunch has learned.

item.title: Warner Bros Shareholders Approve Paramount's $81 Billion Takeover
item.author: BeauHD
item.content:encoded:
Warner Bros. Discovery shareholders have approved Paramount Skydance's takeover bid, moving the massive Hollywood merger a step closer to completion. It's not a done deal quite yet, though, as it still faces regulatory scrutiny and fierce opposition from critics who warn it will further concentrate media power. The Associated Press reports: Per a preliminary vote count Thursday, Warner Bros. Discovery said the overwhelming majority of its stakeholders voted in support of selling the entire business to Skydance-owned Paramount for $31 a share. Including debt, the deal is valued at nearly $111 billion based on Warner's current outstanding shares. That means Warner-owned HBO Max, cult-favorite titles like "Harry Potter" and even CNN could soon find themselves under the same roof with Paramount's CBS, "Top Gun" and the Paramount+ streaming service.
David Zaslav, CEO of Warner Bros. Discovery, said in a statement that stockholder approval marks "another key milestone toward completing this historic transaction." Paramount added that it looks forward to closing in the coming months, and "realizing the creation of a next-generation media and entertainment company." [...] Meanwhile, Warner shareholders rejected a separate measure Thursday outlining post-merger payments for company executives.
